title: Rolling Windows: Smoothing the Path
concept: Rolling windows calculate statistics (like average or maximum) over a fixed window of recent time steps (e.g., past 7 days) to capture short-term trends and smooth out noise.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In time series, if you want to use the value from one step ago to predict the current step, what feature are you creating?
exercises.explain: Correct! Shifting a value from one step ago creates a lag feature (specifically, Lag 1).

cards.title: The noise of daily spikes
cards.body: Imagine you run a restaurant. On Monday you serve 50 guests. On Tuesday, a tour bus stops by and you serve 200. On Wednesday, it's back to 60. If your predictive model only looks at yesterday's numbers, it will wildly over-predict or under-predict. We need a way to smooth out these random daily spikes.

cards.title: What is a Rolling Window?
cards.body: A rolling window (or moving window) takes a fixed number of past time steps (like the last 7 days) and calculates a summary statistic (like the average, maximum, or standard deviation) over just those steps. As time moves forward, the window slides, always keeping the same size.

cards.title: A passenger train window
cards.body: Imagine looking out of a window on a moving train. You can only see the scenery directly in front of the window (e.g., the last 5 miles of track). As the train moves, old scenery leaves your view and new scenery enters. You always see exactly a 5-mile 'slice' of the journey.

cards.title: Calculating a 7-day average
cards.body: Instead of just using yesterday's sales, a store calculates a 7-day rolling average. For Friday's prediction, it averages sales from last Saturday through Thursday. This average absorbs the random spikes and dips, giving the model a stable baseline of recent performance.

cards.title: It doesn't remember forever
cards.body: You might think a rolling window captures all past history. In fact, a rolling window has 'short-term memory'. A 7-day rolling window completely forgets any data older than 7 days. If there was a huge sales spike 8 days ago, the window won't see it at all.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the window size in a rolling window feature as time moves forward?

options.text: The window size remains fixed (e.g., always 7 days).
The window size grows to include all history.
The window size shrinks to focus only on the most recent step.
The window size resets to zero every week.

exercises.explain: Correct! A rolling window always maintains a constant, fixed number of steps.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Using a rolling average helps to smooth out temporary spikes (noise) in your time series data.
exercises.explain: True! Rolling averages smooth out short-term fluctuations, revealing the underlying trend.

exercises.sentence: A rolling window calculation that takes the highest value in the window is called a rolling ___.
exercises.explain: A rolling maximum finds the highest value within the window range.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a rolling window feature?

options.text: The average temperature of the last 3 days, updated daily.
The total number of visitors since the website launched last year.
A flag indicating whether today is a federal holiday.

exercises.explain: Correct! The last 3 days is a fixed-size window that slides forward each day.

exercises.prompt: If you have a daily dataset and create a 30-day rolling standard deviation feature, what is this feature measuring?

options.text: How much the values have fluctuated (volatility) over the past 30 days.
The exact value from 30 days ago.
The average value of the last 3 days.
The overall trend direction (upward or downward).

exercises.explain: Exactly! Standard deviation measures variation. A rolling standard deviation tells the model how volatile or unstable the values have been over the fixed 30-day window. Next: how to capture repeating calendar cycles!
